title: ELU: Exponential Linear Unit
concept: ELU smooths out negative inputs with a curved (exponential) shape instead of a straight line, helping push average outputs closer to zero, which speeds up learning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what makes PReLU different from Leaky ReLU?

options.text: PReLU's slope for negative inputs is learned during training, not fixed
PReLU has no negative inputs at all
PReLU only works in the output layer

exercises.explain: Right — PReLU learns its slope. But Leaky ReLU and PReLU both still use a straight line for negative inputs. What if that line curved instead?

cards.title: Straight lines aren't the only option
cards.body: ReLU, Leaky ReLU, and PReLU all handle negative inputs with a straight line — just with different slopes. But activation functions don't have to use straight lines at all. What if the negative side bent into a smooth curve instead?

cards.title: ELU: a curve for negative inputs
cards.body: ELU (Exponential Linear Unit) keeps positive inputs unchanged, just like ReLU. But for negative inputs, instead of a straight sloped line, it uses a smooth curved (exponential) shape that gently flattens out and levels off near a small negative value, instead of dropping in a straight line forever.

cards.title: A ramp that curves into a landing
cards.body: A straight-line slope (like Leaky ReLU) is a ramp that keeps descending forever, the further negative you go. ELU's curve is more like a playground slide that bends and flattens near the bottom, gently leveling off instead of continuing downward without limit.

cards.title: ELU levels off, it doesn't keep dropping
cards.body: As inputs get more and more negative — -1, -5, -20 — a straight-sloped function like Leaky ReLU keeps outputting more and more negative numbers, without limit. ELU's curve, however, flattens out and gets closer and closer to a small fixed negative number, no matter how negative the input gets.

cards.title: The curve isn't just for looks
cards.body: You might assume ELU's curved shape is just a fancier-looking version of Leaky ReLU with no real benefit. In reality, that curve helps push the network's average output closer to zero (rather than staying solidly positive, like ReLU tends to), which in practice can help a network learn faster.

left.text:
Straight line for negative inputs
Keeps dropping without limit

right.text:
Smooth curve for negative inputs
Levels off near a small negative value

exercises.prompt: How does ELU handle negative inputs differently from Leaky ReLU?

options.text: It uses a smooth curved (exponential) shape instead of a straight line
It outputs exactly 0, same as regular ReLU

exercises.explain: Right — ELU replaces the straight negative-side line with a smooth exponential curve.

exercises.prompt: True or false: as inputs get more and more negative, ELU's output keeps dropping without any limit, just like a straight-line slope.
exercises.explain: False! ELU's curve flattens out and levels off near a small negative value instead of dropping forever.
ELU = Exponential Linear Unit, named for its curved negative side.

exercises.prompt: Which activation function's negative-input shape is a smooth curve that levels off, rather than a straight sloped line?
exercises.explain: ELU uses a curved, exponential shape for negative inputs instead of a straight line.

exercises.prompt: A team wants their network's average neuron output to sit closer to zero (rather than skewing solidly positive like ReLU tends to produce), hoping it'll help the network learn a bit faster. Which activation function is built for exactly this, and how does it achieve it?

options.text: ELU, because its smooth curved shape for negative inputs pushes average outputs closer to zero instead of a straight-line slope or a hard cutoff at 0
Regular ReLU, because chopping negatives to 0 already centers outputs around zero
Softmax, because probabilities always average to zero
PReLU, because learnable slopes always produce curves

exercises.explain: Exactly! ELU's curve helps center outputs closer to zero. So far every activation function we've covered uses a straight edge or a single curve. Next up: two newer functions built differently — smooth from end to end, and self-gated.
